Welcome to the=C2=A01,138=C2=A0newly Not Boring people=C2=A0who have joined=
 us since last Tuesday! If you haven=E2=80=99t subscribed, join=C2=A0189,36=
8 smart, curious folks by subscribing here:=20
Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B,
Happy Monday! After a hectic start to the year, we=E2=80=99re back in the M=
onday slot hitting your inbox with 12k words to kick off the workweek and i=
t feels good.
Back in September, I asked Twitter for their most magical tech experiences =
of the past year. In second place, sandwiched between generative art and cr=
ypto wallets, two topics I=E2=80=99ve written a bunch about, was the produc=
t I use more than any other: Arc by The Browser Company [ https://substack.=
com/redirect/6b9b6b4a-164a-49a4-ab67-afe63b4e17c2?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb=
8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] (you can skip the waitlist with=
 that link, btw).
After I saw the poll results, and wrote a little bit about Arc in Indisting=
uishable from Magic [ https://substack.com/redirect/0facddd0-d764-46c3-85c1=
-b93dd78c7742?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8Ex=
kRNpU ], I knew I wanted to write a full piece about the company. Originall=
y, the main question I had in mind was: why would such a talented team with=
 the ability to craft such a delightful product waste their prime years bui=
lding a browser? Chrome has the market on lock. I viewed their efforts as a=
n amazing contribution to consumer surplus.=C2=A0
But when The Browser Company=E2=80=99s CEO Josh Miller reached out to thank=
 me for the kind words about Arc and offering to explain the company=E2=80=
=99s big vision, I took him up on it, and about three minutes into our call=
 (a good, old fashioned phone call), I knew this piece was going to be much=
 bigger than Arc.=C2=A0
Since the launch of the first modern browser, Mosaic, thirty years ago, the=
 browser has been the turf on which some of tech=E2=80=99s biggest battles =
have been fought. Given its place in the tech stack as the window between t=
he user and the World Wide Web, it=E2=80=99s a powerful demand traffic cont=
roller and a unique beneficiary of all of the innovation that goes on benea=
th its surface. And even when the Wars seem won, when Chrome seems locked i=
n as Internet Explorer did before it and Netscape Navigator did before that=
, something shifts in the internet landscape and opens the door for new com=
petitors with a fresh approach.=C2=A0
I think we might be in one of those moments right now. With AI as a catalys=
t, the giants are signaling their intent to do battle in the browser once m=
ore. But I, of course, am rooting for the underdog with the freshest produc=
t: The Browser Company. Theirs is the Internet Computer I hope everyone is =
using when the dust settles.=C2=A0
This is not a Sponsored Deep Dive, the Browser Company isn=E2=80=99t paying=
 me, and I=E2=80=99m (sadly) not an investor; I just genuinely love the pro=
duct [ https://substack.com/redirect/0facddd0-d764-46c3-85c1-b93dd78c7742?j=
=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] and thi=
nk that the Third Browser War is one of the most interesting stories in tec=
h that not enough people are talking about. It reveals the giants=E2=80=99 =
strategies - their strengths and weaknesses, packages up the history of the=
 internet, and hints at the way billions of people will spend hours every d=
ay in the near future.=C2=A0
Let=E2=80=99s get to it.
Today=E2=80=99s Not Boring is brought to you by=E2=80=A6 Masterworks [ http=
s://substack.com/redirect/000e5598-ffc1-4cf6-ad58-bf8274464a92?j=3DeyJ1Ijoi=
OXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ]
As we=E2=80=99ll cover today, everything is moving to the cloud. That=E2=80=
=99s true of obvious things like apps and data, but it=E2=80=99s true of le=
ss obvious, more beautiful things, too=E2=80=A6 like art.=C2=A0
I don=E2=80=99t own much great physical art, other than a set of vibrant Ta=
kashi Murakami prints that a friend gave to me. I can=E2=80=99t afford the =
good stuff. But on the internet, I own paintings by Basquiat, Haring, Warho=
l, and even Picasso. Well, I own shares in those paintings at least, thanks=
 to Masterworks [ https://substack.com/redirect/000e5598-ffc1-4cf6-ad58-bf8=
274464a92?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNp=
U ].
I=E2=80=99ve been working with Masterworks for years, and personally invest=
ing with them the entire time. In the beginning, before they=E2=80=99d sold=
 any of the pieces they=E2=80=99d purchased, I just thought it was a really=
 cool way to access an inaccessible and less correlated asset class while b=
eautifying my portfolio. Over the past year, though, they=E2=80=99ve begun =
selling works in the portfolio for a gain. In fact, every single one of Mas=
terworks=E2=80=99 11 exits to date has returned a profit to their investors=
, totaling more than $30 million in payouts.=C2=A0
A majority of those sales weren=E2=80=99t in the go-go era of =E2=80=9Cever=
ything goes up=E2=80=9D, meme stocks, and bitcoin at 60k=E2=80=A6 but while=
 inflation soared to decade highs, and financial markets plummeted. They=E2=
=80=99re proving out their thesis: that art can perform well even when othe=
r assets don=E2=80=99t.=C2=A0
New offerings are launching every week, and tend to sell out fast. But as a=
 longtime partner I=E2=80=99ve secured some VIP passes to skip their waitli=
st at the Not Boring link:
See important Regulation A disclosures here [ https://substack.com/redirect=
/d1fa3038-f4c5-4bdc-a5f7-74da3f9b2ed9?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlN=
uaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ].
Internet Computers
There may be no application better positioned to benefit from the rush of r=
apid innovation we=E2=80=99re swimming in than the humble browser.
I say humble, because admit it: you take your browser for granted. The brow=
ser has become so synonymous with the internet, and the internet has become=
 so enmeshed with our lives, that it=E2=80=99s become like the water in tha=
t David Foster Wallace speech [ https://substack.com/redirect/1b48572f-46c8=
-4700-82bc-3680fb7ae581?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbq=
Osa8-6m8ExkRNpU ]:
There are these two young fish swimming along and they happen to meet an ol=
der fish swimming the other way, who nods at them and says =E2=80=9CMorning=
, boys. How=E2=80=99s the water?=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
And the two young fish swim on for a bit, and then eventually one of them l=
ooks over at the other and goes =E2=80=9CWhat the hell is water?=E2=80=9D
What the hell is browser? Like a surfer who paddles with the strength of th=
e gods, the browser has both created and ridden the biggest technological w=
ave in history.=C2=A0=C2=A0
A stunning 5.3 billion people [ https://substack.com/redirect/04b9eca7-c5dc=
-42ca-b47f-7310524a5832?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbq=
Osa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] use browsers to access the wonders of the World Wide Web,=
 an enormous number that has grown 24% since 2019. One-third of the Earth=
=E2=80=99s population, 2.7 billion people, are yet to come online. When the=
y do, rest assured, they=E2=80=99ll access it through a browser, whether on=
 their phone, their desktop, or some futuristic hardware.
This is a piece about which browser they might use, and how.=C2=A0
Browser usage is a relatively recent phenomenon, a decade more recent than =
Excel [ https://substack.com/redirect/ecc98a3b-5760-4081-b945-70687800aff5?=
j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ]. When =
Marc Andreessen launched Mosaic in January 1993, less than 1% of the tiny n=
umber of netizens used a browser. Six months in, 100,000 people connected t=
o the internet through Mosaic. By December of that year, Mosaic counted 1 m=
illion nerds as its earliest users. By the time Andreessen=E2=80=99s next b=
rowser company, Netscape, IPO=E2=80=99d in 1995, there were 16 million peop=
le online. Nine years later, Facebook debuted to an internet audience of 74=
5 million. The next year, in 2005, the internet crossed the 1 billion mark.=
 Now, 5.3 billion of us live online.
Over that same period, the time we spend in browsers has increased with the=
 browser=E2=80=99s capabilities. I just braved my Screen Time Report and di=
scovered that each week, I spend roughly one day=E2=80=99s worth of hours i=
n my browser. I have an especially online job, and it=E2=80=99s hard to fin=
d broad browser usage data to back up my personal anecdata, but one way to =
ballpark it is by thinking about all the things that browsers can do today =
that they couldn=E2=80=99t before.=C2=A0
If one of the most remarkable things about Excel is that it=E2=80=99s thriv=
ed despite the unbundling of its peripheral functionality into hundreds of =
specialized apps=E2=80=A6=C2=A0
=E2=80=A6 then perhaps the most incredible thing about the browser is how m=
uch of our digital activity it=E2=80=99s been able to inhale.=C2=A0
In fact, as Excel and other desktop applications have gotten unbundled and =
turned into cloud-based SaaS products, the browser has been a major benefic=
iary. The biggest tech acquisition of the past year =E2=80=93 Adobe=E2=80=
=99s now-contested $20 billion acquisition of Figma =E2=80=93 is a win for =
the browser. All of the =E2=80=9CFigma for X=E2=80=9D companies that follow=
ed in its wake are a win for the browser. Products like Replit [ https://su=
bstack.com/redirect/4c799208-517f-4449-b9f2-16b91718d65a?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3=
QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] that bring the IDE onlin=
e are a win for the browser.=C2=A0
Any time an application moves from your dock to a tab, that=E2=80=99s a win=
 for the browser.
The browser is this incredible application that seems to only get stronger =
with each new tech cycle. When the vast majority of users interact with web=
3, they do it through the browser. AI has been an unmitigated boon for the =
browser, one that will only accelerate as intelligence moves up a layer, fr=
om walled apps to the browser itself.=C2=A0
As Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella put it at the Bing AI reveal event in Februa=
ry, =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re going to have this notion of a co-pilot that=E2=
=80=99s going to be there across every application canvas, inside of an ope=
rating system shell, in a browser.=E2=80=9D
An =E2=80=9Coperating system shell=E2=80=9D is a technical term. The shell =
is the part of the OS that users interact with, on top of the hardware and =
kernel and below applications. But as Nadella, the CEO of the company that =
has made more money from operating systems than any other in world history,=
 hints, the OS itself is becoming an empty shell. The action is moving to t=
he browser.
This idea =E2=80=93 that we=E2=80=99ll no longer need traditional operating=
 systems, that we=E2=80=99ll just access cloud software through the browser=
 on whichever screen is closest at hand =E2=80=93 is what The Browser Compa=
ny [ https://substack.com/redirect/28b676e5-d719-4475-a743-8ac1711f50ab?j=
=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] CEO Jos=
h Miller [ https://substack.com/redirect/5806243e-e656-460c-aa37-e68db4327b=
3b?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] cal=
ls =E2=9C=A8 Internet Computers =E2=9C=A8.=C2=A0
In Internet Computer World, you don=E2=80=99t need to download apps on your=
 desktop or your phone. They all live in the browser, and the browser becom=
es the OS. And unlike Chrome, the world=E2=80=99s most popular browser, the=
 Internet Computer stays in sync across all of your devices. Across any dev=
ice.=C2=A0
If you want to present on the nearest TV screen, you just log into your Int=
ernet Computer and pull up the presentation tab.=C2=A0
If you start a Not Boring essay on your computer and you have to go to work=
, you can pick up right where you left off on your phone.=C2=A0
The device itself is relatively unimportant in Internet Computer World =E2=
=80=93 it=E2=80=99s commoditized. Anything with a screen and an internet co=
nnection will do. =E2=80=9CIf you smash the computer in front of you right =
now,=E2=80=9D Josh told me, =E2=80=9Cyou wouldn=E2=80=99t lose anything.=E2=
=80=9D The hardware itself becomes the empty shell for the browser; the bro=
wser is how you pull all your stuff down from the cloud.=C2=A0
The browser has had an unparalleled thirty year run. While Excel Never Dies=
, the Browser Keeps Getting Stronger. And now, the stars are aligned for br=
owsers to wipe out traditional operating systems once and for all.=C2=A0
In Differentiation, I wrote:
Things like Replit, Urbit, APIs, and generative AI are only going to accele=
rate the ease with which people can build something, and exacerbate the cha=
llenges for startups building undifferentiated products. Fleshing out where=
 value accrues in this world probably deserves its own post.
This is classic Clayton Christensen stuff, namely The Law of Conservation o=
f Attractive Profits: =E2=80=9CThe law states that when modularity and comm=
oditization cause attractive profits to disappear at one stage in the value=
 chain, the opportunity to earn attractive profits with proprietary product=
s will usually emerge at an adjacent stage.=E2=80=9D
I think the value from the app battle will emerge at two stages: below and =
above. Fierce competition at the app layer will create value that can be ca=
ptured at the infrastructure layer (below) and interface layer (above).=C2=
=A0
We=E2=80=99ll talk about the infrastructure layer in another post =E2=80=93=
 think chips, cloud providers, APIs, and protocols =E2=80=93 and we=E2=80=
=99ll focus on the interface layer (above) in this one. =E2=80=9CHe who con=
trols the interface, controls the universe.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
Just who gets to control the Browser du Jour is another question: The Brows=
er Wars have been fought across decades, in software and courtrooms, among =
tech=E2=80=99s biggest players. Today, Google Chrome dominates the space, w=
ith an absurd 65.7% market share. But every ten years or so, a new Browser =
War begins, and Nadella=E2=80=99s renewed interest in the browser =E2=80=93=
 Microsoft Edge is a name I=E2=80=99d heard maybe twice in the past five ye=
ars until a month ago =E2=80=93 signifies the beginning of a new Browser Wa=
r.=C2=A0
The Browser Wars are not for the faint of heart. To win, you need to compet=
e against a three-headed monster of the world=E2=80=99s largest and most po=
werful tech companies.=C2=A0
The battlefield is littered with smaller foes: Mosaic, Netscape, Mozilla, O=
pera, Flock. The Browser Company is the latest in a long line of hopefuls.=
=C2=A0
The new Browser War is such a compelling topic because it hits so many rich=
 veins:
Storied Past: The history of the browser is the history of the internet=C2=
=A0
Surfing Waves: The browser gains strength from each new tech trend, and the=
 latest, AI, is pulling more activity into it.=C2=A0
Commoditizing Complements: As the battles beneath the browser intensify, it=
 (literally) sits above the fray.=C2=A0
Tech Giant Strategy: Each giant has its obvious strengths and weaknesses th=
at are ripe for Counter Positioning.=C2=A0
An Even Brighter Future: With a new business model, the Internet Computer i=
s potentially the most valuable place in the digital value chain.
There are two big questions to ask here:=C2=A0
Are Internet Computers the Future?=C2=A0
Who Will Build the Winning Internet Computers?=C2=A0
We=E2=80=99ll dive into all of this and more. To read on, click the link:
Thanks to Josh and Darin for your input, and to Dan [ https://substack.com/=
redirect/51836e6d-642a-4d12-9a8b-104a09e1f4ac?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T=
7GnA_ZlNuaMZjmlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU ] for editing!
That=E2=80=99s all for today! We=E2=80=99ll be back in your podcast feeds t=
his week and your inbox on Friday for that Weekly Dose of Optimism.
Thanks for reading,
Packy

</em><span>? Chrome has the market on lock. I viewed their efforts as an am=
azing contribution to consumer surplus. </span></p><p style=3D"color: =
#404040; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">But when =
The Browser Company’s CEO Josh Miller reached out to thank me for the=
 kind words about Arc and offering to explain the company’s big visio=
n, I took him up on it, and about three minutes into our call (a good, old =
fashioned phone call), I knew this piece was going to be much bigger than A=
rc. </p><p style=3D"color: #404040; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">Since the launch of the first modern browser, Mosaic=
, thirty years ago, the browser has been the turf on which some of tech&#82=
17;s biggest battles have been fought. Given its place in the tech stack as=
 the window between the user and the World Wide Web, it’s a powerful =
demand traffic controller and a unique beneficiary of all of the innovation=
 that goes on beneath its surface. And even when the Wars seem won, when Ch=
rome seems locked in as Internet Explorer did before it and Netscape Naviga=
tor did before that, something shifts in the internet landscape and opens t=
he door for new competitors with a fresh approach. </p><p style=3D"col=
or: #404040; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;">I thi=
nk we might be in one of those moments right now. With AI as a catalyst, th=
e giants are signaling their intent to do battle in the browser once more. =
But I, of course, am rooting for the underdog with the freshest product: Th=
e Browser Company. Theirs is the Internet Computer I hope everyone is using=
 when the dust settles. </p><p style=3D"color: #404040; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 20px 0;"><span>This is not a Sponsored De=
ep Dive, the Browser Company isn’t paying me, and I’m (sadly) n=

ddd0-d764-46c3-85c1-b93dd78c7742?j=3DeyJ1IjoiOXAwZ3QifQ.gb8J5T7GnA_ZlNuaMZj=
mlXXepKbqOsa8-6m8ExkRNpU" rel=3D"" style=3D"color: #404040; text-decoration=
: underline;">genuinely love the product</a><span> and think that the Third=
 Browser War is one of the most interesting stories in tech that not enough=
 people are talking about. It reveals the giants’ strategies - their =
strengths and weaknesses, packages up the history of the internet, and hint=
s at the way billions of people will spend hours every day in the near futu=
re. </span></p><p style=3D"color: #404040; font-size: 16px; line-heigh=
